name: Copilot Prompt Conditioning Generates Targeted Synthetic Chatbot Inputs
statement: Watts et al. use Microsoft Copilot 365 prompts conditioned on high-quality examples, low-quality examples, and typographical-error instructions to generate synthetic student inputs for chatbot prompt testing.
population_or_context: Middle-school scientific argumentation chatbot prototype evaluation.
quantitative_note: The article reports 18 synthetic student inputs and 162 chatbot responses across prompt versions.
caveats: The generated text is chatbot input, not a scored student response corpus.
The source does not validate generated inputs against real student response distributions.
The finding is boundary evidence for prompt-conditioned stress testing, not direct evidence of synthetic response realism.
evaluation_method: Synthetic inputs were used to test multiple chatbot system prompt versions and evaluate chatbot responses.
reported_metrics: 18 synthetic student inputs; three independent chatbot responses per input for each prompt version, totaling 162 chatbot responses.
limitations: The generation context is scientific argumentation chatbot design.
Prompt-conditioned input generation is not equivalent to automated scorer validation or general student-response simulation.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the Copilot prompt-conditioning description and reported input/response counts.
